data.title: Genius Sports: Strong Top Line Growth Masks Fragile Economics
data.description:
Introduction

Genius Sports (GENI) is a middleman that sits behind the scenes of the sports ecosystem as a data and technology provider. It collects real time data from live games through tracking systems and partnerships with leagues, then packages and distributes that data to sportsbooks, media companies, and teams. Their core product is "official" data, which is faster and more reliable than unofficial sources and is especially valuable for live betting and broadcast enhancements. On top of raw data, the company has its own products like BetVision (interactive streaming with integrated betting) and various analytics and fan‑engagement tools. Those generate revenue and help it embed itself across multiple points in the sports value chain.

The business primarily makes money through signing contracts with leagues to secure exclusive data rights and then reselling that data to customers like betting operators and broadcasters at a markup. They also generate revenue from advertising services that help leagues and brands reach fans. In theory, this creates a recurring revenue model because customers depend on their data feeds and technology to operate.

In practice though, this is not a particularly attractive business if you dig deeper which is why I've got a more cautious stance on the stock. The most important asset (the underlying data) ultimately belongs to the leagues. That means Genius has to continually pay up to maintain access and is always at risk of losing key contracts to competitors like Sportradar. That creates recurring bidding pressure which caps margins. Even when Genius wins, it often does so on terms that require sharing more of the economics with rights holders or accepting lower profitability to defend its market position.

Investment Thesis

My investment thesis on Genius Sports is that the competitive and macro realities make the current narrative around platforms like BetVision and the post‑Legend media business look overly smooth. It seems operators are becoming more disciplined about marketing and vendor spend as sports betting matures which could weigh on the pace at which Genius can push pricing or cross‑sell higher‑margin services across its betting and media portfolio. As I'll explain in this article, the media and advertising goals with Legend introduce also carry risk because ad budgets are highly sensitive to economic conditions. Keep in mind too that ad‑tech is a crowded field where newer entrants struggle to become "must‑buy" partners. All in, I'm of the view Genius looks more fragile and volatile than the headline growth and guidance might suggest which is why I rate shares a 'sell'.

A look at Genius Sports' 2025 results

When looking at the latest Q4'25 and FY'25 results for Genius Sports, the company delivered a strong year with revenues of $669.5 million, up 31% year over year with momentum across segments as Betting Technology rose 33% to $471.5 million on higher existing-customer penetration and expansion of BetVision (now covering NFL, Serie A, FIBA Basketball, and dozens of soccer, tennis, and e-sports events). In the Media Technology climbed 37% to $144 million with new agency ties with PMG, Publicis, and WPP. The company also saw ad growth across 600 NBA games via NBC Sports Regional Networks, and a near-doubling of 2H'25 revenue compared to last year.

In my view, the Q4's revenue surge to $240.5 million (up 37%) was pretty strong (especially in Media) and the company's Americas growth of 41% exceeded Europe at 20%. Revenue came in $5.6 million ahead of sellside estimates, however EPS of -$0.02 missed sellside estimates by 15 cents.

When looking at the company's profitability, EBITDA climbed 59% to $136.2 million for the year, clocking in at a 20.4% margin. This level was the company's highest since going public and was driven by operating leverage that came from scaling high-margin tech products and services. All of that seemed to be in spite of continued investments in rights and innovation through the P&L. On surface, it would look like evidence of maturation of the company's business model but as I'll discuss later, I think margins are approaching a peak. Moreover, there were also items unadjusted for like high stock-based compensation (tied to the extended NFL deal) and litigation-related expenses that show a wide gap between GAAP earnings and adjusted EBITDA.

On the balance sheet, Genius Sports closed out the year with $281 million of cash on the books which was roughly double where it was last year. But most of this came from an equity raise where the company issued 17.6 million shares at $8.50 for $144 million in net proceeds back in January. Based on commentary from the conference call following the quarter, management noted that this was being done in conjunction with Legend's acquisition which is expected to close in Q2. Given that the company has no debt, I might have preferred the company to use a bit of leverage to finance the transaction. But at $8.50, I don't think the shares were particularly cheap so I can understand and can't fault them for this decision.

Outlook

As I'll discuss in the valuation section, when one looks at Genius Sports valuation on TTM numbers, it's easy to say that the company trades at a cheap valuation and a lot of the negatives seem priced in. After all, 2025 was a good year and revenues and profitability were higher.

On the conference call following the quarter, management guided for 2026 revenues to fall in the range of $810-820 million, implying 22% growth with EBITDA climbing 26% to the $180-190 million range. Assuming the Legend acquisition closes, the company should be able to get to annual revenues of $1.1 billion in revenue and management assumes EBITDA of $320-330 million in EBITDA, roughly 30% margins with FCF conversion at nearly 50%.

But in my view, I think management's guide for Genius Sports looks meaningfully too optimistic. The guidance for 2026 is for low‑20s topline growth and a strong step‑up in EBITDA assumes that 2025's momentum rolls forward into a fundamentally less forgiving environment for both operators and ad buyers. I don't see a lot of room for positive surprises on the P&L.

Risks to Genius Sports are numerous

The reason for this is that the core betting and data side is likely more late‑cycle risk than management is acknowledging. In my view, the U.S. sports betting market is clearly maturing. Shown below, one data point to support this is that the American Gaming Association (AGA) saw in January that US sports betting handle (the total amount wagered) declined year over year for the first time since legalization when excluding the 2020 pandemic anomaly.

The way I ultimately see this playing out is that promotion intensity will fade, operator focus will shift from gross handle to contribution profit, and the incremental operator dollar will be squeezed harder for ROI. In the near future, Genius's ability to push pricing, upsell new services, and expand share is likely constrained by the fact that its customers themselves are under pressure to cut variable costs and renegotiate vendor contracts.

As operators like DraftKings (DKNG) and FanDuel shift their focus from gross handle to contribution profit, they are indeed looking at their largest variable costs: Data Rights. We're already starting to see this play out as operators increasingly push for capped data fees or tiered pricing rather than a pure percentage of revenue. On Draftkings' earnings call last month, the company's CFO highlighted their focus on an "efficient and powerful business model" that focuses on structural hold (the % of bets the house keeps) and promotional discipline rather than just raw volume.

If the operators' growth slows, Genius's revenue share model hits a ceiling. As well with promotional intensity down (TV ad units for betting fell 9% in 2025), there is less "new money" entering the system. That means Genius has to find growth by cannibalizing existing budgets.

Where I could be wrong

While Genius Sports will argue the strategic value of official data, they ultimately do not own the rights. I do question the assumption that exclusivity automatically translates into smooth revenue growth when operators are re‑basing their cost structures.

At the same time, I also acknowledge that the Media Technology business is growing well. But even then, Genius' business model is tough to navigate going forward as they try to move from being a "tax" on betting volume to being an "advertising engine." By acquiring companies like Legend, management is attempting to own the audience as opposed to just providing the data. If they successfully pivot to a high-margin advertising platform, they may escape the vendor squeeze I have concerns about.

The media and advertising business still carries risk though. The bull case of the stock is that Genius Sports is ultimately going to be able to successfully stitch together data, audience, and CTV/streaming inventory into something that sits closer to performance budgets. It's what's needed to stabilize the business long term but it's also a crowded part of the ad tech stack. And one where if the macro wobbles and ad budgets are the first to get cut, it's rarely the biggest incumbent pipes that take the hit first.

Cheap valuation on the surface, but beware of adjustments

The sellside is in my opinion too optimistic on the outlook for Genius Sports. With 16 'buy' ratings and only 2 'hold' ratings, analysts are firmly in the bullish camp with an average price target of $11.72. This implies about 149% upside from current levels (source: Bloomberg)

When looking at the valuation, Genius Sports is inexpensive on EBITDA at 4.0x their estimate. But historically, this has been a lower margin business and given my expectation that operating leverage will remain elusive as the cost of securing exclusive data rights (like the NFL extension through 2029) rises in tandem with revenue, I think the EBITDA figure could be quite a bit lower.

Moreover, investors need to need to treat it with a grain of salt given management's own guidance that FCF conversion is likely going to be below 50% and stock-based compensation is a real expense that's not included in EBITDA. For 2025, Genius Sports paid $148.5 million in stock based comp.

For these reasons, even though the company's valuation has fallen out of bed at 4.0x EV/EBITDA, I still think this is a story that carries a fair bit of risk and that adjusted EBITDA overstates the true earnings power of the business.

Bottom line

Genius Sports has clearly demonstrated strong top line growth but I am skeptical that these gains are as durable as they appear. The company operates in a structurally challenging position where it does not control its core asset, faces ongoing pricing pressure from both leagues and customers, and is attempting a pivot into a crowded and cyclical advertising market. When you layer in maturing sports betting dynamics and limited room for execution missteps, the path to sustaining high growth and expanding margins looks far less certain than management's guidance implies. For me, the current valuation does not adequately compensate for these risks which is why I rate shares of Genius Sports as a 'sell'.

data.title: Lithium Miners News For The Month Of March 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the March 2026 edition of the lithium miner news.

The past month saw lithium prices generally flat.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported a Australian spodumene concentrate 6% Li2O CIF China spot price of USD 2,080, as of March 24, 2026, roughly flat the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price was flat last month, now at CNY 147,500.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5 year chart - CNY 147,500 (~USD 21,399) (source)

Lithium demand versus supply outlook

Katusa Research is forecasting a small lithium deficit to begin in 2026 (as of January 2026) (source)

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028, may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

Albemarle is forecasting lithium deficits more likely from ~2027/28 (as of mid 2025) (source)

GGII, a Shenzhen-based consultancy, forecasts a balanced lithium market this year, a 10,000t LCE deficit in 2026, and a 60,000t deficit in 2027 (as of mid 2025) (source)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (Trend Investing) (IEA)

Lithium market and battery news

On February 25 Seeking Alpha reported:

Lithium stocks rise as Zimbabwe bans exports of lithium concentrates, raw minerals...The export ban, which is intended to compel mining companies to refine minerals in the country, is effective immediately and will remain in place until further notice, Zimbabwe's mining minister said...Zimbabwe has some of the largest lithium reserves in Africa and is among the top global producers, with resources estimated at 126M tons, according to official data. The country's ban on lithium concentrates had been expected to come into effect in 2027 as part of a push for more local processing.

On February 26 Car News China reported:

New breakthrough in lithium battery technology enables 700 Wh/kg energy density...Compared to traditional oxygen-coordinated electrolyte systems, fluorinated hydrocarbon solvents offer superior wettability and utilisation efficiency, significantly reducing the required amount of electrolyte. Furthermore, the weaker lithium-fluorine coordination enables rapid charge transfer processes even at low temperatures.

On February 27 Investing News Network reported:

UK enters commercial lithium production with geothermal plant launch. The British Geological Survey has estimated that UK demand for lithium could increase between 12 and 45 times during the 2020s.The UK has entered commercial lithium production for the first time as Geothermal Engineering Ltd (GEL) began operations in its plant at Cornwall, anchoring the government's hopes of a domestic battery metals supply chain...Initial production is set at 100 tons per year, with plans to expand to 1,500 tons annually within several years and to more than 18,000 tons over the next decade. That long-term expansion would require an estimated £640 million, or around US$860 million, in additional investment.

On February 28 Bloomberg reported:

Lithium recovery has legs as demand outlook improves, SQM says. Top lithium producer SQM expects prices for the battery metal to extend gains as demand strengthens and the industry emerges from a global glut.

In March Fastmarkets reported:

Lithium: Tightening supply expectations combined with macroeconomic uncertainty causes volatility...Asian carbonate prices rocketed back above $20 per kg, tracking surging GFEX futures contracts as the market tried to process news of export controls in Africa. Zimbabwe’s lithium export ban sparks price surge and industry shifts. Zimbabwe is forecast to supply 124,000 tonnes of LCE in 2026, around 7% of global output, and accounts for about 15% of China’s spodumene imports. Huayou Cobalt currently operates a lithium sulfate processing plant, with Sinomine Resource Group and Sichuan Yahua Industrial Group currently developing capacity. Following the news of the ban, spodumene prices reacted sharply, reaching $2,430–$2,500 per tonne in late February... sentiment remained cautious amid underwhelming reported EV sales in early 2026 and geopolitical uncertainty affecting energy storage projects in the Middle East. Rising lithium prices have also begun to cause concerns for ESS projects, where the recent price rally has seen a 10–15% increase in system costs...The fundamentals do not appear to have significantly altered from the previously held view of a tighter market in early 2026, with demand outpacing supply.

On March 9 Bloomberg reported:

CATL shares rally on profit beat fueled by energy storage boom...Net income climbed 42% in 2025 from a year earlier to 72.2 billion yuan ($10.4 billion), ahead of estimates, the Chinese company said late Monday. Revenue rose 17% as more overseas shipments and a burgeoning energy-storage business helped offset cooling demand from China’s EV market... The revenue of CATL’s energy storage segment jumped by about 9% from the prior year, while total revenues from the company’s overseas segment rose about 18%. International expansion remains a core pillar of CATL’s growth ambitions, despite the headwinds it faces from geopolitics.

On March 17 Bloomberg reported:

LG says it will supply Tesla with $4.3 billion of batteries.

  • LG Energy Solution Ltd. will produce batteries in Michigan for Tesla Inc.'s energy storage systems business.
  • Production at the lithium-iron-phosphate prismatic battery cell plant is scheduled to begin next year.
  • The batteries will power Tesla's Megapack 3 energy storage systems produced in Houston, creating a domestic battery supply chain.

Lithium miner news

Albemarle (ALB)

On March 16 Albemarle announced: "Pricing for cash debt tender offers...to purchase up to $650 million aggregate principal amount of the Notes."

On March 23 Albemarle posted:

Grid Energy Storage 101: What it is and why it matters...according to Albemarle analysis, global energy storage system production is forecast to grow 25 to 60 percent year over year in 2026, underscoring the technology’s expanding role in the future energy system...According to the U.S. Department of Energy, data centers can consume 10 to 50 times more electricity per floor space than a typical commercial office building.

Sociedad Quimica y Minera S.A. (SQM), Covalent Lithium (SQM/WES JV) - Wesfarmers [ASX:WES] (OTCPK:WFAFY)

The Mt Holland Lithium Mine and Kwinana Refinery are a 50/50 JV ("Covalent Lithium") between Wesfarmers and SQM, located in Western Australia.

On February 27 SQM announced:

SQM reports earnings for the twelve months ended December 31, 2025.

  • SQM reported total revenues for the twelve months ended December 31, 2025 of US$4,576.2 million compared to total revenues of US$4,528.8 million for the same period last year.
  • Net income for the twelve months ended December 31, 2025 of US$588.1 million or US$2.06 per share, compared to net loss of US$(404.4) million or US$(1.42) per share for the same period last year.
  • In lithium: Record quarterly sales volumes amidst strong market demand.
  • Strong price environment in iodine.

"We continue to observe solid demand fundamentals, and we estimate that the lithium market could grow by approximately 25% this year, led by electric vehicles (EVs) and ESS."

Rio Tinto [ASX:RIO] [LN:RIO] (RIO) - Includes a 53.9% stake in Nemaska Lithium in a JV with the Quebec government

On March 10 Seeking Alpha reported:

Rio Tinto ships first lithium from Argentina's Rincon project, wins $1.17B financing package...Rio Tinto (RIO) said Monday it made its first commercial shipment of lithium carbonate produced at the Rincon project in Argentina, marking the formal start of exports from the mine...Rincon is expected to add an annual production capacity of 53K metric tons of battery-grade lithium carbonate, starting in 2028 with 25K tons.

On March 13 Rio Tinto announced:

Update on the Nemaska Lithium Bécancour project...Rio Tinto and the Nemaska Lithium Board have agreed to slow the pace of construction during 2026 to bring together the full project expertise of Nemaska Lithium and Rio Tinto...Engineering for the Bécancour facility has been completed, and construction is now more than 70% advanced... Construction is expected to ramp up again once the optimisation work has been completed, and Rio Tinto does not foresee major changes to the project’s overall timeline.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2026 - Plan to expand and ramp Fenix Mine by 10ktpa and start and ramp Sal de Vida Project to 15ktpa LCE. Total lithium 2026 production target is 61-64kt LCE.
  • 2028 - Plans to ramp Rincon to 25ktpa, then later build Rincon up to 53ktpa LCE; and start Nemaska Lithium Project then ramp to 28ktpa LCE.

Note: You can view a nice summary of all Rio's lithium projects and timelines here. Their end 2028 target is 225,000tpa LCE.

Ganfeng Lithium Group [SHE:002460] [HK:1772] (GNENY)

No significant news for the month.

(Chengdu) Tianqi Lithium Industries Inc. [SHE:002466], Tianqi Lithium Energy Australia (TLEA) is a JV with Tianqi Lithium (51%) and IGO Limited (49%). TLEA owns the Kwinana lithium hydroxide facility in WA

No significant news for the month.

Chengxin Lithium Group Co Ltd [SHE:002240] - Also owns Sichuan Zhiyuan Lithium Industry Co., Ltd and is a supplier to BYD Co.

On March 19 Market Screener reported:

Beijing tells Chinese firms to strengthen Zimbabwe risk prevention after mineral export ban...Africa's top lithium producer announced the suspension of mineral exports on February 25, citing alleged malpractices and leakages. Chinese mining firms including Zhejiang Huayou Cobalt, Sinomine, Chengxin Lithium Group Yahua and the Tsingshan Holding Group dominate Zimbabwe's lithium and chrome mining sectors.

Pilbara Minerals [ASX:PLS] (OTC:PILBF)

No significant news for the month.

Mineral Resources [ASX:MIN] (MALRF)

Mineral Resources core Australian lithium assets include the Mt Marion Mine and the Wodgina Lithium Mine JV (ownership subject to final settlement in H1, 2026 - MinRes 35%, POSCO 15%, Albemarle 50%).

No significant news for the month.

Sigma Lithium Resources [TSXV:SGML] (SGMLF) (SGML)

Sigma is producing lithium spodumene at its Grota do Cirilo Mine in Brazil.

On March 20 Sigma Lithium announced:

Sigma Lithium resumes sales of high-grade premium lithium oxide; closes inaugural sale at plant of 400,000t of high-purity low grade fines expecting a US$20 million profit...In 1Q26, the Company is expected to generate gross revenues equivalent to the sale of approximately 28,000 tonnes of high purity premium lithium oxide concentrate at a grade-adjusted price of US$1,712 per tonne.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026/27 - Second line to be commissioned doubling production capacity to 520,000tpa spodumene (~80,000t LCE) once ramped. A 2027/28 third stage plans to expand the mine to 120,000tpa LCE.

Liontown Limited [ASX:LTR] (OTCPK:LINRF) - formerly Liontown Resources Limited

Liontown 100% own the Kathleen Valley Lithium (spodumene) Mine in Western Australia.

On March 12 Liontown Limited announced:

Half-Year Financial Report for the period ended 31 December 2025. Production scaling as underground transition completes and balance sheet resets. Highlights:

  • 192,514 dmt produced at 5.0% Li₂O (up 70%); 189,596 dmt sold.
  • Underground ore mined 533 kt across 31 stopes — 1 Mtpa run-rate delivered on schedule. Targeting 1.5 Mtpa by end March 2026.
  • Revenue $207.5M (up 107%). Underlying EBITDA $(7.7)M reflecting ramp-up phase with unit costs declining through the half.
  • Statutory loss $(184.0)M includes $(104.4)M non-cash LGES derivative charge, which will not recur following the conversion to equity in February 2026.
  • Unit operating costs A$985/dmt (FOB); AISC A$1,179/dmt (FOB). FY2026 guidance unchanged.
  • LGES convertible note converted to equity in February 2026. Pro forma gearing excluding leases 22% (from 48%). Nil net gearing excluding leases. Cash $390.5M.
  • 4 Mtpa expansion refresh underway.

AMG Critical Materials N.V. [NA:AMG] [GR:ADG] (OTCPK:AMVMF)

On March 16 AMG announced:

AMG Critical Materials N.V. publishes 2025 Annual Report...While both of our key markets, lithium and vanadium, continued with depressed price levels, AMG’s strategic positioning, both across critical materials and within each respective value chain, once again allowed us to deliver a strong adjusted EBITDA performance, exceeding 2024 by 40%. Our performance validates a central thesis of critical materials markets, namely that enduring value creation is anchored in process technology leadership and portfolio diversification.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026-2028 - German LiOH facility expansion plan with Modules 2-5 (100,00tpa LiOH).

Elevra Lithium [ASX:ELV] (NASDAQ:ELVR) - Formed from a merger of Sayona Mining & Piedmont Lithium

On February 26 Elevra Lithium announced:

FY26 Half Year Results...NAL produced 96,156 dry metric tonnes (dmt) of spodumene concentrate during the FY26 half year, reflecting continued stable operations, with production 7% lower than the prior corresponding period...Spodumene concentrate sales totalled 91,991 dmt across three shipments at an average realised price (FOB) of US$937 per dmt...The Group profit after income tax of US$74 million for the half year reflected a US$116 million increase from the PCP due to improved underlying EBITDA and benefit from extraordinary items...Closing cash balance of US$81 million...Across our growth portfolio, we continued to advance environmental, permitting and development activities for Moblan, Ewoyaa and Carolina Lithium during the six-month period to maintain momentum and progress key milestones.

Lithium Argentina AG [TSX:LAR](LAR) - formerly Lithium Americas Argentina

Lithium Argentina owns an effective 44.8% share of the Cauchari Olaroz JV, also a 33% share in the Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes lithium brine project JV. The key partner in both is Ganfeng Lithium.

On March 11 Lithium Argentina announced:

Lithium Argentina expands Cauchari-Olaroz Resource estimate; Supports Stage 2 development plan...Expanding on the Stage 1 operating capacity of 40,000 tonnes per annum (“tpa”) of lithium carbonate equivalent (“LCE”), Stage 2 targets an additional 45,000 tpa of LCE...increased Measured and Indicated (“M&I”) resource estimate by 42% to 28.1 Mt of LCE.

  • Updated M&I resource with an average grade of 562 mg/L lithium (up from 19.9 Mt LCE at 592 mg/L previously).
  • Inferred resource increased to 9.6 Mt LCE at an average grade of 567 mg/L lithium (up from 4.7 Mt LCE at 592 mg/L previously).

Results of an updated Stage 2 development plan are expected in mid-2026.

On March 23 Lithium Argentina announced:

Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 Results. Net Income: For the fourth quarter of 2025 was $31 million, and for the year ended December 31, 2025, was a loss of $39 million5...2026 production guidance set at 35,000 - 40,000 tonnes of lithium carbonate.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026 - Stage 2 Cauchari-Olaroz is advancing an expansion plan considering an additional production capacity of 45,000 tpa LCE. (Stage 1 capacity is at least 40,000tpa).
  • 2026-28 - Progress on the Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes lithium brine project JV (Ganfeng 67%, LAR 33%) with a Scoping Study for up to 150,000tpa LCE.

NB: Ganfeng Lithium (51%) and Lithium Argentina (49%) own the JV company Minera Exar S.A., which owns 91.5% interest and is entitled to 100% of the production from the Cauchari-Olaroz Project. The 8.5% interest is owned by Jujuy Energia y Mineria Sociedad del Estado (“JEMSE”) (a company owned by the Government of Jujuy province).

Zijin Mining Group [SHA:601899] [HKSE:2899] (ZIJMF)

Zijin Mining is a large diversified mining group with global mines focused on copper, gold, zinc/lead, silver and lithium. Zijin Mining owns 100% of the 3Q Lithium Mine in Argentina, with Stage 1 production started producing 2,903t LCE in 2023 and planning to ramp to 25,000tpa+ LCE. Zijin has other lithium assets and very large production growth plans.

On March 20 Zijin Mining announced:

Net profit of US$7.4 billion for 2025; Total social contribution reaches US$16 billion...The company delivered strong operational performance and significant profitability gains, achieving revenue of US$49.7 billion, up 15% year on year, and net profit attributable to shareholders of the parent company of US$7.4 billion, up 62%...In 2025, the company produced 90 tonnes of mined gold, representing a 23% year‑on‑year increase—one of the highest growth rates among major global miners. This growth was driven in part by two world‑class operations acquired during the year: the Akyem Gold Mine in Ghana and the Raygorodok Gold Mine in Kazakhstan. Mined copper output reached 1.09 million tonnes, growing from an already high base. Three lithium projects—the Lakkor Tso Salar in Xizang, the Tres Quebradas Salar in Argentina, and the Xiangyuan Hard-Rock Lithium Mine in Hunan—were all put into production, bringing the company’s production of lithium carbonate equivalent (LCE) to 25,500 tonnes and marking a step change from limited output to large‑scale production. According to the company’s guidance for 2026, it plans to produce 105 tonnes of mined gold, 1.2 million tonnes of mined copper, 120,000 tonnes of LCE, and 520 tonnes of mined silver.

Eramet [FR:ERA] (OTCPK:ERMAY) (OTCPK:ERMAF)

Eramet owns the Centenario-Ratones Lithium Mine in Argentina.

No lithium related news for the month.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • End 2026 - Ramp up of Phase 1 lithium production in Argentina to full capacity of 24ktpa LCE. Long term plan is to expand to above 75ktpa LCE.

POSCO [KRX:005490] (PKX)

POSCO owns the Sal de Oro Project in the Hombre Muerto salar, Argentina. Some details here. POSCO also has a JV with Pilbara Minerals on a 43ktpa lithium hydroxide facility in South Korea which commenced operations in 2024. Deal to acquire 30% of Mineral Resources share of WA assets (Wodgina and Mt Marion).

No lithium news for the month; however there was some rare earths news.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2026 - Ramp up production at Hombre Muerto and ramp to 25ktpa LiOH, then later an additional 25ktpa lithium carbonate. Acquire the Hombre Muerto North Lithium Project from Lithium South Development Corp.

Core Lithium Ltd. [ASX:CXO] [GR:7CX] (CXOXF) (CORX)

Core 100% owns the Finniss Lithium Project (Grants Resource) in Northern Territory Australia. The Project is currently not in production due to previous low prices.

On March 18 Core Lithium announced:

Final investment decision and funding secured for the restart of the Finniss Lithium Operation...Fully funded restart package includes strategic support (Strategic Funding) of US$120 million (A$170 million1) in aggregate from a consortium comprising of Glencore Australia Holdings Pty Limited (Glencore), InfraVia CMF Invest S.à r.l. (InfraVia) and Nebari Natural Resources Credit Fund II, LP (Nebari) (together Strategic Partners), and an intended A$120 million (before costs) equity raise.

On March 19 Core Lithium announced:

Successful $120 million Institutional Placement as part of Finniss Final Investment Decision..."With funding now secured, we can move immediately into mobilisation, early works and development activities to position Finniss for first concentrate production in the September quarter of 2026."

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • September quarter 2026 - Finniss restart targeted for first concentrate production.

Argosy Minerals [ASX:AGY][GR:AM1] (ARYMF)

Argosy Minerals 77.5% owns (option to increase to 90%) the Rincon Lithium Mine in Argentina. Their 2,000tpa lithium facility is currently suspended.

On March 6 Argosy Minerals announced:

12KTPA RINCON LITHIUM PROJECT UPDATE.

  • Process technology test works produce 96% lithium recovery.
  • High purity lithium chloride concentrate successfully produced.
  • Lycopodium engaged to progress Rincon 40MW energy infrastructure works.

The current feasibility works are focused on 12ktpa Stage 1a, being the production of solid lithium chloride product on site.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026+ - Work on the next stage (total 12,000tpa) project at Rincon Lithium Project. Any re-opening of the suspended 2,000tpa operations at Rincon Lithium Mine.

Other Chinese lithium producers (several are not listed)

  • Zhicun Lithium Group Ltd (private)
  • Sichuan Yahua Industrial Group Co Ltd [SHE:002497]
  • Jianxiawo (owned by CATL)
  • Jiangxi Nanshi Lithium Battery New Material Co., Ltd (owned by Nanshi Group and Yichun Mining)
  • Jinzhou Yongshan Lithium [SHA:603399]
  • Sinomine Resource Group [SHE:002738]
  • Canmax [SHE:300390]
  • Huayou Cobalt [SHA:603799]

Lithium miner ETFs

  • Sprott Lithium Miners ETF (LITP) - A pure play lithium ETF
  • Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT)
  • ProShares S&P Global Core Battery Metals ETF (ION)
  • The Amplify Lithium & Battery Technology ETF (BATT)

Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT) 5 year price chart (source)

Trend Investing lithium demand v supply model forecasts

Trend Investing forecasts lithium demand to increase 10.6x this decade.

Note: A Nov. 2020 UBS forecast is for "lithium demand to lift 11-fold from ~400kt in 2021 through to 2030."

Conclusion

China lithium carbonate spot prices and spodumene spot prices were generally flat the past month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • Lithium stocks rise as Zimbabwe bans exports of lithium concentrates.
  • New Chinese breakthrough in lithium battery technology enables 700 Wh/kg energy density, using fluorinated hydrocarbon solvents.
  • UK enters commercial lithium production with geothermal plant launch in Cornwall, initial production is set at 100 tons per year.
  • Bloomberg: Lithium recovery has legs as demand outlook improves, SQM says.

data.title: Geospace Technologies Painful, But Necessary Transformation
data.description:
In my last thesis on Geospace Technologies (GEOS), I referred to the “Geospace paradox”, noting that the company is a story in two acts: what it is today, and what it is building toward. In the first act, it was a Houston-based designer and manufacturer of seismic instrumentation for the oil and gas sector, “a chronic stock market underperformer, trapped in the boom-and-bust cycle of energy exploration”, and yet able to generate the cash flows and technical credibility that made the second act possible. The second act is now well underway, and it is decidedly more interesting. Three segments, Energy Solutions, Smart Water, and Intelligent Industrial, sit under one roof, each at a different stage of maturity, and the sum of their parts is only beginning to be understood by the market. Even so, I noted that, “in the last five years, the company has become a stock market winner, handily beating key benchmarks”. However, the company was, in my last thesis, “priced for perfection”, leading me to assign a “Hold” rating. 1Q26 was unambiguously difficult, showing that perfection was not attainable. In this thesis, I re-iterate that “Hold” rating, noting that the near-term pain should not obscure the medium-term opportunity. This update examines what happened, why it happened, and what the road ahead looks like.

An Ugly 1Q26

On 4 February 2026, Geospace reported 1Q26 revenue of $25.6 million, a 31.3% decline year-over-year from $37.2 million in 1Q25. Net loss came in at $9.8 million, or -$0.76 per diluted share, compared to net income of $8.4 million, or $0.65 per share, in the same period over the previous year. Over the same period, gross profit plunged 86.6% to $2.7 million, and the operating loss widened to $10.2 million, a deterioration of 230.8% year-over-year. The stock duly slumped.

A segmented breakdown of the financial performance reveals massive shortcomings particularly in Energy Solutions which the CEO Rich Kelley notes is a segment “defined by uncertainty and change”.

Three factors drove the decline, and all three are identifiable and to varying degrees transient. Energy Solutions, which was down 40% year-over-year (“YoY”) to $14.6 million, experienced a prior year quarter $17 million OBX marine wireless product sale. This was an unusually large single transaction. The sales to Dawson Geophysical which began in 3Q25 are meaningful, but smaller. OBX rental fleet utilization also fell. That unusual, lumpy sale, more than any underlying deterioration, drove the segment's decline. In addition, tariffs, and inflationary pressures have converged to make what Kelley called, “difficult operating environment”. The Smart Water segment, down 21% YoY to $5.8 million, continues to operate in what Kelley describes as “stable, yet increasingly demanding environment”, driven by population growth and urbanization. This is an era of smart water infrastructure. That said, near-term revenue fluctuations reflect the episodic nature of utility procurement cycles. Finally, the Intelligent Industrial segment, which was down 8% YoY to $5.1 million, has experienced lower demand for industrial sensor products. The segment is the steadiest of the three, with contract manufacturing and imaging products providing a predictable floor.

The Petrobras Contract Offers Growth

The single biggest development in the Geospace investment case over the past year has little to do with the 1Q26 miss. In June 2025, Geospace announced a landmark $90 million Permanent Reservoir Monitoring (PRM) contract awarded by Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (PBR) for the Mero Fields 3 and 4 in the Santos Basin, 180 kilometres off the coast of Rio de Janeiro, in Brazil.

The contract covers the engineering, procurement, construction, and operation of nearly 500 kilometres of Geospace’s fibre-optic sensing system across 140 square kilometres of seabed.

Revenue is recognized using a percentage-of-completion methodology and is expected to begin in 3Q26 until completion, with installation services, in 1Q27.

That Petrobras, one of the world’s largest oil companies, decided to give this contract to Geospace, is a sign of the quality of the company’s solutions. The contract is transparent, and milestone-driven, and lends predictability to the company’s revenue stream based on a defined delivery schedule. Considering that Geospace has a trailing twelve-month (“TTM”) revenue of $99 million, a $80 million contract is quite a leap. Geospace is also in discussions with “multiple” other, as-yet undisclosed companies regarding additional PRM opportunities.

A Small, But Important Acquisition

In July 2025, Geospace acquired GeoVox Security, the exclusive licensee of a human heartbeat detection algorithm developed by the U.S. Department of Energy's Oak Ridge National Laboratory. The Heartbeat Detector uses proprietary geophone sensors to identify concealed individuals in vehicles by detecting the micro-vibrations a person’s beating heart produces. Oak Ridge, Sandia, and Thunder Mountain national laboratories have proven that the technology has a 99% effectiveness rate and a 10-second scan time. The technology is currently deployed in over a dozen countries, serving prison security, border checkpoints, and human trafficking prevention.

As the GeoVox deal was completed in mid-2025, the anticipated revenue will only begin to hit Geospace’s income statement this quarter, as Kelley explained in the 1Q26 earnings call.

“So we'll have revenue recognition in this quarter, which we'll announce at the next call, the next release. We anticipate a couple of hundred units in this year, and then that building over the subsequent periods. There is a tremendous amount of interest, both domestically and internationally, in that.”

As yet, the financial impact of this acquisition has been negligible. In fact, the Intelligent Industrial segment, of which GeoVox forms a part, has had a decline in revenue YoY from $5.5 million in 1Q26 to $5 million in 1Q25. However, segment losses have also narrowed.

The addressable market is rather large, with about 10,000 global prison facilities, over 300 manned U.S. border checkpoints, and more than 600 European border crossings. With the prospect of large revenues comes competition, and GeoVox faces stiff competition from ENSCO's MicroSearch system, which offers a contactless vehicle sensor option; UK-based ClanTect, whose Motion Detection Technology is deployed by the UK Border Force, Frontex, and HM Prison Service; and Austria's Mira Technologies, whose AI-powered Magus HBD system is used across EU border agencies. Chinese manufacturers such as WaveSonic Technology and Securina Detection offer comparable products at lower prices.

In response to the rising competitiveness of the market, Geospace has introduced a monthly subscription model for the Heartbeat Detector. This allows government customers to purchase orders under operating budgets rather than getting capital expenditure approval. In the 1Q26 earnings call, management announced that shipments have begun, with a few hundred units expected in 2026 and several thousand units possible over time. The degree of customer interest exceeds that which GeoVox experienced in the past. The monthly subscription model should both increase revenues and make them more predictably recurring. In so doing, the Intelligent Industrial segment will act as a ballast to the lumpy, project-driven cycles of the Energy Solutions segment.

Valuation Presents Downsides

At the current price, Geospace has a price/sales of 1.4. In comparison, the Seeking Alpha peer group, which is made up of Oceaneering International, Inc. (OII), Oil States International (OIS), Cactus, Inc. (WHD), Forum Energy Technologies (FET), and NCS Multistage Holdings, Inc. (NCSM), carries an average P/S of 1.5. The sector median, meanwhile, is 1.75, according to Seeking Alpha. Aswath Damodaran’s data suggests a price/sales sector median of 0.57 for oilfield services and equipment.
